meta.title: Understanding ClaimFarm
meta.subtitle: A deep, code-grounded tour of the whole codebase
meta.promise: Learn the real ClaimFarm codebase subsystem by subsystem: channels, agents, AI, RAG, the console, auth, data, and how to run and extend it.
outcomes: Trace one photo from a verified webhook to a filed claim, through the real functions
Explain each of the nine agents and the client it calls, with file citations
Describe the security posture: signature checks, rate limiting, headers, Argon2id, RBAC
Name where every kind of data lives and how it is retrieved
Swap a provider (vector store, payments, identity) or run and deploy the app

c-story.name: What ClaimFarm is
c-story.summary: A chat-to-claim autopilot with a human checkpoint for smallholder crop insurance.

c-problem.name: The problem and product
c-problem.summary: A two-sided SaaS: farmers on chat, adjusters on a console; the win is filing conversion.

c-channels.name: Channels and webhook trust
c-channels.summary: Three providers, three signature schemes, one shared pipeline, fail-closed.

c-hardening.name: Rate limiting and headers
c-hardening.summary: Per-IP limiter on /api and /auth plus OWASP security headers on every response.

c-intake.name: The intake orchestrator
c-intake.summary: process_inbound runs the synchronous pipeline and always replies to the farmer.

c-vision.name: Multimodal vision
c-vision.summary: Qwen-VL returns a strict-JSON DamageAssessment, a typed function from an LLM.

c-forensics.name: Photo forensics
c-forensics.summary: EXIF, perceptual hashing, and a VL authenticity check, defense in depth.

c-weather.name: Weather corroboration
c-weather.summary: Open-Meteo history aggregated into drought/flood signals that test the damage claim.

c-rag.name: Vector store and RAG
c-rag.summary: One embedding, two jobs: retrieve precedents and detect near-duplicate fraud.

c-output.name: Drafting and localization
c-output.summary: Qwen-Max drafts, WeasyPrint renders a PDF, replies are translated templates.

c-review.name: The adjuster console
c-review.summary: get_claim assembles evidence; post_decision files to the insurer and notifies.

c-authz.name: Auth and RBAC
c-authz.summary: Argon2id passwords, opaque server-side sessions, role hierarchy, audit log.

c-data.name: Data and storage
c-data.summary: SQLModel records, OSS blobs, DashVector embeddings, each store by shape.

c-extend.name: Provider abstractions
c-extend.summary: Vector store, payments, and identity are swappable behind interfaces via config.

c-deploy.name: Running and deploying
c-deploy.summary: uv, Docker, Function Compute, Vercel, CI dual-push, with real startup quirks.

c-tradeoffs.name: Tradeoffs
c-tradeoffs.summary: Honest demo shortcuts, each with a named production fix.

t-qwenvl.name: Qwen-VL-Max
t-qwenvl.purpose: Reads the crop photo into a structured DamageAssessment.

t-qwenmax.name: Qwen-Max
t-qwenmax.purpose: Drafts claim text and translates farmer replies.
t-qwenmax.tradeoffs: Cheaper than vision rates for text-only work.

t-embed.name: text-embedding-v3
t-embed.purpose: Embeds claims for similarity search and fraud.
t-embed.tradeoffs: 1024-dim; one representation serves RAG and fraud.

t-fastapi.name: FastAPI + Starlette
t-fastapi.purpose: Webhooks, the console API, middleware, and BackgroundTasks.

t-argon2.name: argon2-cffi
t-argon2.tradeoffs: OWASP 2023 default; legacy PBKDF2 still verified and rehashed.

t-vector.name: DashVector / Chroma
t-vector.purpose: Vector search behind one VectorStore interface.
t-vector.tradeoffs: DashVector in prod, Chroma for local dev via get_store().

t-openmeteo.name: Open-Meteo
t-openmeteo.tradeoffs: Free and keyless, removing a credential.

t-weasyprint.name: WeasyPrint + Jinja2
t-weasyprint.purpose: Renders the claim PDF from an HTML template.

t-oss.name: Alibaba OSS
t-oss.purpose: Stores photos and PDFs with signed URLs.
t-oss.tradeoffs: Keeps bytes out of the app process.

t-fc.name: Function Compute 3.0
t-fc.tradeoffs: Cheap for bursty demos; SQLite in /tmp is ephemeral.

s-readme.note: product surface and stack table (orientation only)

s-main-seed.note: ephemeral-SQLite demo seed on cold start

s-claimsrepo.note: save + index_claim into the vector store

s-docker.note: CMD execs uvicorn from the venv

g-hmac.definition: A keyed hash a sender attaches so the receiver can prove the request is genuine and unmodified.

g-webhook.definition: A URL a service calls when an event happens, like an inbound chat message.

g-argon2.definition: A memory-hard password hashing algorithm; the OWASP 2023 default.

g-rbac.definition: Role-Based Access Control: what you may do depends on your role.

g-rag.definition: Retrieval-Augmented Generation: fetch relevant facts and feed them to the model.

g-embedding.definition: A vector capturing meaning so similar items sit close together.

g-phash.definition: A fingerprint of an image's look; near-identical images get near-identical hashes.

g-mor.definition: A payment provider that sells on your behalf and handles tax and compliance.

g-hitl.definition: The system prepares the work but a person makes the final call.

g-idempotent.definition: Safe to run more than once with the same result; matters because webhooks retry.

chk-story.q: What makes ClaimFarm's design acceptable to insurers?
options: A human adjuster approves before filing
chk-story.why: A human-in-the-loop adjuster approves each claim before it is filed, which insurers require.

chk-problem.q: Which metric captures the product's value?
options: Share of eligible losses that become reviewed claims
chk-problem.hint: What failed before was filing at all.
chk-problem.why: The wedge is filing conversion: turning losses that never got filed into reviewed claims.

chk-channels.q: How is a Twilio webhook proven genuine?
options: A shared password in the URL
HMAC-SHA1 over the URL + sorted params, checked in _verify_twilio_signature
chk-channels.why: _verify_twilio_signature recomputes HMAC-SHA1(auth_token, url+sorted params); Bird uses HMAC-SHA256, Telegram a secret header.

chk-failclosed.q: If a channel's signing secret is unset, the webhook...
chk-failclosed.why: An unconfigured secret makes the endpoint reject, so a misconfiguration never opens the door to forged claims.

chk-rate.q: What does IPRateLimiter protect and how?
options: /api and /auth, ~60 requests per IP per minute via a sliding window
chk-rate.why: It keeps a per-IP deque of timestamps, drops expired ones, and returns 429 past 60/min on /api and /auth.

chk-headers.why: It sets X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, CSP, HSTS and removes the server header; never a password.

chk-intake.q: If persisting the claim fails, process_inbound...
options: still saves best-effort and still replies to the farmer
chk-intake.why: It degrades to a best-effort save and always sends the reply, prioritizing farmer experience over strict consistency.

chk-vision.q: How is Qwen-VL output made safe to use in code?
options: A strict JSON prompt parsed into a typed DamageAssessment
chk-vision.why: A strict JSON, conservative prompt yields a typed DamageAssessment, turning the model into a reliable typed function.

chk-forensics.q: Why does forensics combine EXIF, perceptual hashing, and a VL check?
options: Defense in depth: each catches a different kind of fake
chk-forensics.why: EXIF (metadata), perceptual hash (reuse of a known image), and a VL authenticity check each detect a different manipulation.

chk-weather.q: How does summarize_window flag a likely drought?
chk-weather.why: It aggregates Open-Meteo daily data into signals like consecutive_dry_days and days_with_heavy_rain to test the damage cause.

chk-rag.q: How does one embedding serve both RAG and fraud?
options: Similar past claims are helpful precedents and near-duplicate risk from the same search
chk-rag.why: The same similarity surfaces useful precedents for the adjuster and suspicious near-duplicates for fraud_check.

chk-vector.q: How are Chroma and DashVector interchangeable?
options: Both implement the VectorStore ABC; get_store() picks one from config
chk-vector.why: VectorStore is an abstract base; ChromaStore and DashVectorStore implement it and get_store() returns one based on settings.vector_store.

chk-output.q: Why translate templated status messages instead of free-generating replies?
options: To bound what the bot can say so it never overpromises a payout
chk-output.why: Translating fixed templates keeps farmer-facing copy controlled, avoiding accidental payout promises.

chk-review.q: What does get_claim add on top of the stored claim?
options: Top-3 similar past claims and fraud flags
chk-review.why: get_claim calls past_claim_rag.find_similar(k=3) and fraud_check.check, bundling evidence for one decision.

chk-authz.q: What happens to a legacy PBKDF2 password on next login?
options: Verified, then transparently rehashed to Argon2id (needs_rehash)
chk-authz.why: verify_password accepts legacy PBKDF2 and needs_rehash triggers an upgrade to Argon2id on successful sign-in.

chk-data.q: Why move from SQLite to Postgres in production?
options: On Function Compute SQLite lives in ephemeral /tmp and is wiped on recycle
chk-data.why: FC's /tmp is ephemeral, so SQLite data does not survive; DATABASE_URL points at Postgres for durability.

chk-extend.q: How do you switch identity or payment providers?
options: Swap the implementation behind the Protocol/interface, selected by config
chk-extend.why: identity_verification and payments define interfaces with multiple implementations chosen by settings, so swapping is config, not code.

chk-deploy.q: Why does the Dockerfile CMD exec uvicorn directly instead of 'uv run'?
options: 'uv run' re-syncs the project at startup, slowing cold starts
chk-deploy.why: Calling the venv's uvicorn avoids uv re-resolving project metadata on every Function Compute cold start.

d-system.title: System map
d-system.code:
flowchart LR
  TG[Telegram] --> WH[Webhooks + verify]
  WA[WhatsApp] --> WH
  WH --> MID[Rate limit + headers]
  MID --> PIPE[Agent pipeline]
  PIPE --> VL[Qwen-VL-Max]
  PIPE --> QM[Qwen-Max]
  PIPE --> OM[Open-Meteo]
  PIPE --> DV[(DashVector)]
  PIPE --> DB[(SQLModel)]
  PIPE --> OSS[(OSS)]
  WEB[Next.js console] --> API[api_claims]
  API --> DB

d-pipeline.title: The intake pipeline
d-pipeline.code:
flowchart TD
  IN[verified payload] --> LANG[detect_language]
  LANG --> DMG[assess_damage Qwen-VL]
  DMG --> FOR[photo_forensics]
  FOR --> WX[weather corroborate]
  WX --> DRAFT[build_claim]
  DRAFT --> PDF[render_claim_pdf]
  PDF --> SAVE[save + index_claim]
  SAVE --> REPLY[localized status_message]
  SAVE --> REVIEW[adjuster console]
  REVIEW --> EV[find_similar + fraud_check]

d-auth.title: Role hierarchy
d-auth.code:
flowchart LR
  owner --> admin --> moderator --> reviewer --> farmer

d-data.title: Where data lives
d-data.code:
flowchart TD
  C[Claim] --> SQL[(SQLModel record)]
  C --> OSS[(OSS photo + PDF)]
  C --> DV[(DashVector embedding)]
  A[Every decision] --> AUD[(append-only audit log)]

children.name: storage
children.role: repos, db, audit log, photo store

children.name: tests
children.role: pytest suite incl the pipeline test

modules.title: Orientation
modules.summary: What it is and who it serves.

lessons.title: What ClaimFarm is
lessons.summary: A single crop photo over Telegram or WhatsApp becomes a filed insurance claim, with a human approving before it is sent.

sections.what: A multimodal agent pipeline on FastAPI: a messaging webhook runs ordered agents (vision, forensics, weather, RAG, drafting, multilingual), persists a Claim, and surfaces it in a Next.js adjuster console.
sections.why: To collapse a multi-page, multi-language, evidence-heavy form into one photo so far more farmers actually file.
sections.how: process_inbound assesses the photo and replies fast; the adjuster later opens the claim, sees assembled evidence, and approves; post_decision files to the insurer.
sections.connects: Channels in front; Qwen Cloud, Open-Meteo, DashVector, OSS in the middle; a console and insurer API at the end.
sections.ifChanged: Remove the human checkpoint and it becomes automatic payouts, which insurers reject and fraud exploits.

lessons.analogy: Like a tax app that files your whole return from one receipt photo, but an accountant still signs off.
lessons.insight: The orchestrator is deterministic Python, not an LLM planner, so latency and failure modes stay bounded inside a webhook timeout.
lessons.teachBack: Explain ClaimFarm to a teammate in three sentences, naming the two user types.

lessons.title: The problem and the product
lessons.summary: Fewer than 20% of eligible smallholder farmers ever file a claim; ClaimFarm is a real two-sided SaaS that removes the blockers.

sections.what: A conversion problem: eligible losses that never become filed claims because of language, evidence, and literacy barriers.
sections.why: The value is unlocking claims that never happened, not shaving minutes off existing ones.
sections.how: Farmers touch only a chat channel in their own language; adjusters and staff use the console, auth, RBAC, billing, and identity surfaces the repo actually ships.
sections.connects: Every design choice (multilingual replies, photo-as-evidence, a human checkpoint) serves filing conversion.
sections.ifChanged: Target a large insured agribusiness instead and the photo-first chat interface would be the wrong tool.

lessons.example: A farmer who reads only Telugu with no scanner produces a complete, evidence-backed claim from a phone photo.
lessons.teachBack: Explain why '<20% file' makes this a conversion problem, not an accuracy problem.

lessons.title: Channels and webhook verification
lessons.summary: Three messaging providers each call a webhook in main.py; each is verified with a different scheme before any work runs, and all funnel into one pipeline.

sections.what: Routes twilio_inbound, bird_inbound, and telegram_inbound, each with a matching verifier, feeding whatsapp_intake.process_inbound (and its Bird/Telegram variants).
sections.why: The signature check is the trust boundary; without it anyone could POST fake claims into the queue.
sections.how: _verify_twilio_signature recomputes HMAC-SHA1 over url + sorted params; _verify_bird_signature uses HMAC-SHA256 over the raw body; _verify_telegram_secret compares a secret header. Work runs in a BackgroundTask so the 200 returns within the provider timeout.
sections.connects: Each provider payload is normalized to one internal shape, so the agents never care which app the farmer used.
sections.ifChanged: An unconfigured secret makes the route reject (SEC-008 fail-closed), so a misconfiguration can never silently open the door.

lessons.warning: Verification happens before the pipeline; a request that fails the signature check is rejected with 403 and never reaches an agent.
lessons.teachBack: Name the three verification schemes and why unconfigured secrets fail closed.

lessons.title: Rate limiting and security headers
lessons.summary: Two Starlette middlewares protect every request: a per-IP rate limiter on sensitive prefixes and OWASP security headers on every response.

sections.what: IPRateLimiter (a BaseHTTPMiddleware) throttles /api and /auth per client IP; SecurityHeaders adds OWASP headers to every response.
sections.why: Cheap, always-on defenses against brute force and common web attacks, independent of the routes.
sections.how: The limiter keeps a per-IP deque of monotonic timestamps, evicts entries older than the window, and returns 429 with Retry-After past 60/min. SecurityHeaders sets X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, CSP, and HSTS, and strips the server header.
sections.connects: Registered in main.py around the app; a real deployment fronts this with a CDN/WAF for distributed limiting.
sections.ifChanged: Drop the limiter and /auth is open to credential stuffing; drop the headers and the app loses clickjacking and MIME-sniffing protection.

walkthrough.note: A sliding-window counter per IP: evict expired timestamps, reject past the rate.
setdefault means it never overrides headers a route set on purpose.

lessons.teachBack: Explain how the per-IP sliding window works and one header and what it prevents.

lessons.title: Inside process_inbound
lessons.summary: The 894-line whatsapp_intake orchestrates the whole synchronous pipeline and guarantees the farmer always gets a reply.

sections.what: process_inbound downloads the photo, detects language, calls assess_damage, corroborates weather, builds the Claim, renders a PDF, saves and indexes it, and replies in the farmer's language.
sections.why: It is the single ordered place the intake sequence lives, so behavior is easy to reason about.
sections.how: The photo is base64-encoded into a data URL for Qwen-VL; a Farmer is built (a default GPS is used when no location is shared, with a location-share keyboard offered on Telegram); persistence is wrapped in try/except so a save failure still replies.
sections.connects: Calls damage_assessor, weather_corroborator, claim_drafter, multilingual, claims_repo, and the channel client.
sections.ifChanged: Reorder corroborate before assess_damage and the weather step has no damage cause to test against.

lessons.example: No photo in the message and process_inbound short-circuits with a prompt to send one, never running the AI.
lessons.warning: The default GPS is an explicit demo shortcut; production requires an opt-in location share, which the Telegram flow already scaffolds.
lessons.teachBack: List process_inbound's calls in order, from download to reply.

lessons.title: Vision: Qwen-VL to typed JSON
lessons.summary: damage_assessor turns a free-form vision model into a typed function by demanding strict JSON, parsed into a DamageAssessment.

sections.what: assess_damage sends the photo to Qwen-VL with a conservative, JSON-only system prompt and parses the result into the DamageAssessment model.
sections.why: Downstream code branches on fields like severity and confidence, so the output must be structured and trustworthy.
sections.how: qwen.py reaches Qwen via the OpenAI SDK (DashScope is OpenAI-compatible); vision_chat passes the image as an image_url content part with json_mode; the prompt forbids inventing symptoms and says to use low confidence when unsure.
sections.connects: The DamageAssessment feeds weather corroboration (it needs a damage cause) and the adjuster's evidence view.
sections.ifChanged: Drop json_mode and the strict prompt and parsing into DamageAssessment becomes fragile.

walkthrough.code:
SYSTEM_PROMPT = """\
You are a crop insurance damage assessor.
Identify the crop, whether damage is present, the primary cause,
a severity score (0-100), affected area %, and your confidence.
Be conservative. If unsure, say so via low confidence.
Return STRICT JSON only.
"""
walkthrough.note: The prompt is the reliability mechanism: conservative, JSON-only.

walkthrough.code:
resp = client.chat.completions.create(
  model=s.qwen_vl_model,
  messages=[{"role":"user","content":[
    {"type":"image_url","image_url":{"url": image}},
    {"type":"text","text": user_text}]}],
)
walkthrough.note: Same call shape as the OpenAI SDK; only base_url and model change.

lessons.teachBack: Explain how strict JSON output makes an LLM behave like a typed function.

lessons.title: Photo forensics
lessons.summary: Before trusting a photo, forensics stacks three independent checks so no single spoof passes.

sections.what: photo_forensics combines EXIF inspection, perceptual hashing, and a Qwen-VL authenticity prompt into a forensics verdict.
sections.why: A claim is only as trustworthy as its evidence; each check covers a different attack.
sections.how: perceptual_hash computes an average_hash and compares with hamming_distance via find_close_matches to catch reused/known images; EXIF flags missing/edited metadata; the VL prompt flags AI-generated or screenshot content.
sections.connects: Runs on the downloaded bytes inside intake; results surface to the adjuster alongside fraud flags.
sections.ifChanged: Rely on EXIF alone and a re-encoded screenshot (EXIF stripped) would pass; the perceptual hash still catches reuse.

lessons.example: A stock-photo screenshot is flagged by missing EXIF plus a perceptual-hash match even though the crop looks real.

walkthrough.note: Near-identical images differ in only a few bits, so a small Hamming distance means reuse.

lessons.teachBack: Explain why three checks beat one, with a concrete example each catches.

lessons.title: Weather corroboration
lessons.summary: weather.py turns 30 days of Open-Meteo history into drought/flood signals that independently test the damage cause.

sections.what: summarize_window pulls daily weather for the farmer's location and aggregates it into a WeatherSummary; weather_corroborator judges whether it supports the claimed cause.
sections.why: It grounds one model's photo verdict in an independent, keyless data source, reducing reliance on the vision call.
sections.how: It computes totals and counts (days_with_heavy_rain > 30mm, days_above_35c, days_with_frost) and runs like consecutive_dry_days via _max_run over daily precipitation.
sections.connects: The summary is passed with the DamageAssessment into build_claim so the drafted claim reflects corroboration.
sections.ifChanged: A 'drought' verdict is strengthened by many consecutive_dry_days and weakened by high total_precip_mm.

walkthrough.note: The longest streak of dry days is a strong drought signal.

lessons.teachBack: Explain how consecutive_dry_days is computed and why it corroborates a drought claim.

lessons.title: The vector store, RAG, and fraud
lessons.summary: One embedding representation powers both helpful retrieval and similarity-based fraud, behind a swappable VectorStore interface.

sections.what: vector_store defines a VectorStore ABC with ChromaStore (local) and DashVectorStore (prod) implementations; get_store() returns one based on config. past_claim_rag and agronomy_rag query it; fraud_check reads similarity.
sections.why: Abstracting the store means the agents never change when you swap providers, and one representation serves two purposes.
sections.how: Claims are embedded with text-embedding-v3 and indexed on save (claims_repo calls index_claim); find_similar(k=3) retrieves precedents; fraud_check flags near-duplicates by high similarity.
sections.connects: Retrieval results and fraud flags are assembled by api_claims.get_claim for the adjuster.
sections.ifChanged: Empty the past-claims collection and fraud-by-similarity has nothing to compare against, so it must be populated on save.

lessons.insight: Same vectors, two readings: 'similar past claim' is a helpful precedent for the adjuster and a near-duplicate risk for fraud.

walkthrough.note: One interface, two implementations, chosen by config.

lessons.teachBack: Explain how swapping Chroma for DashVector requires no change to the agents.

lessons.title: Drafting the PDF and speaking the language
lessons.summary: Qwen-Max drafts the claim, WeasyPrint renders it to PDF, and farmer replies are translated templates, not free text.

sections.what: claim_drafter.build_claim assembles the Claim and render_claim_pdf renders an HTML/Jinja2 template through WeasyPrint; multilingual detects the language and localizes status messages.
sections.why: The insurer needs a document; the farmer needs an understandable, controlled reply.
sections.how: The PDF is written and stored in OSS with signed URLs; status_message/localize translate fixed templates via Qwen-Max rather than generating each reply.
sections.connects: Consumes the DamageAssessment + WeatherSummary; the PDF path is saved on the Claim for the console.
sections.ifChanged: Free-generate replies and the bot might promise a payout it cannot guarantee, a real risk in a regulated message.

lessons.warning: Replies translate fixed templates, so the model never invents a promise to the farmer.
lessons.teachBack: Explain when the app uses Qwen-VL vs Qwen-Max vs the embedding model, one example each.

lessons.title: The adjuster console decision
lessons.summary: The console reads an assembled evidence bundle and writes exactly one decision that files the claim and notifies the farmer.

sections.what: install_api wires read endpoints (list_claims, get_claim, get_claim_photo, get_localized_reply) and the write endpoint post_decision, all behind require_auth.
sections.why: This is the human-in-the-loop boundary and the read/write split of the whole system.
sections.how: get_claim calls past_claim_rag.find_similar(k=3) and fraud_check.check on demand; post_decision flips ClaimStatus, calls the insurer client, and _notify_farmer sends a localized message. A no-cache middleware keeps the JSON fresh.
sections.connects: Consumed by the Next.js console in web/; lazy evidence computation keeps the farmer's intake reply fast.
sections.ifChanged: Auto-approve high-confidence claims and you remove the fraud backstop the insurer relies on.

lessons.example: Opening claim CF-123 triggers a DashVector lookup for 3 similar claims and a fraud scan, returned in one get_claim response.
lessons.teachBack: Describe everything between the adjuster clicking approve and the farmer being notified.

modules.title: Platform: auth and data
modules.milestone: You can reason about the security posture end to end.

lessons.title: Passwords, sessions, and RBAC
lessons.summary: Argon2id password hashing with transparent legacy upgrades, opaque server-side sessions, and a five-role hierarchy enforced across the API.

sections.what: auth/passwords hashes with Argon2id and still verifies legacy PBKDF2; auth/routes issues opaque server-side sessions; roles run owner > admin > moderator > reviewer > farmer.
sections.why: Credentials and authorization are the app's trust core; getting hashing and role checks right is non-negotiable.
sections.how: verify_password accepts both schemes; needs_rehash upgrades legacy hashes to Argon2id on next login. api_admin.set_role changes roles and every privileged action appends to the audit log.
sections.connects: require_auth gates the console API; api_admin, api_magic_link, and api_keys_routes build on the same session/role model.
sections.ifChanged: Skip needs_rehash and old PBKDF2 hashes never get upgraded, weakening accounts created before the Argon2id switch.

walkthrough.note: OWASP 2023 Argon2id params; legacy PBKDF2 verified then upgraded.

lessons.teachBack: Explain what happens to a legacy PBKDF2 password on a successful login.

lessons.title: Storage, models, and the audit log
lessons.summary: Three stores chosen by data shape, typed models, and an append-only audit trail for every privileged action.

sections.what: SQLModel over DATABASE_URL holds claims/farmers/users/sessions; OSS holds photos and PDFs; DashVector holds embeddings. Models live in app/models; storage/ wraps CRUD.
sections.why: Records, blobs, and semantic search have different access patterns, so each gets the right store.
sections.how: claims_repo.save persists the Claim and triggers index_claim into the vector store; audit_log appends who did what; on Function Compute the SQLite file is in ephemeral /tmp, hence the startup demo seed.
sections.connects: The models (Claim, ClaimStatus, Farmer) are the shared shapes every layer speaks.
sections.ifChanged: Keep SQLite in production and data vanishes on instance recycle; DATABASE_URL points at Postgres for durability.

lessons.teachBack: For one claim, name the three stores its data spreads across and what each holds.

modules.title: Operate and extend
modules.summary: Run it, swap providers, ship it.
modules.milestone: You could add a feature or swap a provider safely.

lessons.title: Swapping providers with config
lessons.summary: Identity, payments, and the vector store are each an interface with multiple implementations chosen by settings, so swapping is configuration, not code.

sections.what: identity_verification defines an IdentityProvider Protocol with MockProvider/PersonaProvider (Veriff, Onfido ready); payments abstracts a merchant of record over Paddle/LemonSqueezy/Razorpay; vector_store swaps via get_store.
sections.why: The demo runs on free/mock providers while the seams to real vendors stay in the codebase for judges and future work.
sections.how: config.Settings reads env (models, vector_store, DATABASE_URL, provider keys, PAYMENTS_PROVIDER=none by default); each subsystem picks its implementation from those settings.
sections.connects: GDPR (export_my_data, delete_my_account) and notifications (multi-channel dispatcher) follow the same abstraction style.
sections.ifChanged: Point INSURER_BASE_URL at a real carrier and set a payments provider and the mocks become production integrations with no agent changes.

lessons.teachBack: Describe how you would switch the identity provider from Mock to Persona.

lessons.title: Running, testing, and deploying
lessons.summary: uv to install, pytest to verify, Docker + Function Compute to run, Vercel for the console, CI dual-pushing the image, with real cold-start quirks.

sections.what: Local: uv sync, fill .env, run uvicorn, run pytest (the suite includes test_claims_pipeline). Deploy: build the image, run on Function Compute (scale-to-zero); the console is on Vercel.
sections.why: Serverless scale-to-zero fits bursty, cheap, demo-friendly hosting.
sections.how: The Dockerfile CMD execs uvicorn directly from the venv (using 'uv run' would re-sync at startup); CI lints/tests, typechecks the web build, and dual-pushes to ghcr.io and Alibaba ACR; FC pulls from ghcr.io.
sections.connects: The startup seed exists because FC's SQLite is ephemeral; tests double as executable documentation of the pipeline.
sections.ifChanged: Use 'uv run' in the CMD and every cold start re-resolves metadata, slowing responses.

lessons.teachBack: Explain how a commit to main becomes a running container on Function Compute.

lessons.title: Tradeoffs and the pitch
lessons.summary: Every demo shortcut is deliberate and has a named production fix; leading with them builds trust.

sections.what: An honest map of what is real (a full SaaS surface + human checkpoint) versus scoped for the demo.
sections.why: Confident, truthful framing wins judges and survives hard questions.
sections.how: Name each limit with its concrete fix, most of them config switches.
sections.connects: The fixes map to real settings: DATABASE_URL, INSURER_BASE_URL, PAYMENTS_PROVIDER, an approved WhatsApp number.
sections.ifChanged: Hide the limits and one pointed question can unravel the pitch.

rows.b: Opt-in location share (Telegram flow already scaffolds it)

lessons.teachBack: Give a 60-second pitch: what it is, the problem, the live path, the human checkpoint, one honest limit and its fix.

lessons.title: Teach it back
lessons.summary: Prove you can walk the whole system, naming real files and functions.

sections.what: A self-assessment across every subsystem you just learned.
sections.why: Contributing means you can navigate the real code, not just the pitch.
sections.how: Narrate a verified webhook, process_inbound, the agents, get_claim/post_decision, and the auth/data platform, citing files.
sections.ifChanged: If any subsystem is fuzzy, revisit that lesson; the dashboard shows which concepts need review.

lessons.teachBack: Write a 4-minute contributor walkthrough citing at least six real files, ending with one limitation and its fix.
